transcript:
Forty-eight warm-colored points drift across the dark field, each one trailing a thin line behind it — and slowly those lines pile up into a tangled web that eventually swallows the whole frame. It's less a scene than a slow accumulation, like watching a single evening turn into geologic sediment.

Here's the part that got me: in the update function, when a wanderer gets close to a line on screen, it has no way of knowing whether that line is its own past or someone else's. Look at the comment right there — "the wanderer cannot distinguish its own evidence from anyone else's." There's even a flag, called "is own trail," that quietly checks the truth behind the scenes — the system knows, but the dot never does. And each wanderer has a trait called "curiosity or flight" that decides whether it orbits the mark to investigate it, or swerves away — pure personality, completely disconnected from memory.

So the flash you see when a dot meets a line isn't recognition. It's curiosity mistaking your own past for a stranger's. The piece never tells the dot which lines are its own — and neither, really, can we.
